does: This paper introduces RULER, a suite of representation-level verification metrics for machine unlearning that probe whether forgotten training records are truly removed from a model's internal representations. The authors develop two key metrics: M2, an oracle-comparative metric that measures whether forget-set records occupy the same representational positions as in a model retrained without them, and M4, an oracle-free metric that detects residual traces of forgotten data from the unlearned model's internal similarity structure alone. Testing four approximate unlearning methods and a fifth method called Bad Teacher that all pass standard output-level evaluations, the paper finds that M2 detects significant representational residuals in 10 of 12 conditions with effect sizes increasing as the forget fraction grows, demonstrating that models can appear to successfully unlearn at the output level while still encoding forgotten information in hidden layers.
connects: This work extends the machine unlearning literature by addressing a critical gap in verification methodology. Prior work established unlearning as a solution to privacy concerns and model correction but relied on output-level verification through membership inference attacks, retain accuracy, and forget-set accuracy metrics. The paper builds on representation-level analysis traditions from interpretability research and extends recent findings that models can satisfy output-level unlearning criteria while retaining information in intermediate representations. It responds to growing concerns about the adequacy of surface-level evaluation protocols and combines insights from privacy research, model interpretability, and linear mixed-effects modeling to provide more rigorous verification standards that other unlearning methods must now meet.
enables: This work establishes representation-level verification as a necessary component of unlearning evaluation, enabling more robust assessment of whether unlearning is genuinely effective. RULER's oracle-free M4 metric enables practitioners to diagnose unlearning quality without access to retrained models, making verification practical in deployment settings. The findings suggest future unlearning methods must be designed with representation-level forgetting in mind, potentially driving development of improved unlearning algorithms that truly remove information across all model layers. The framework also enables auditing of existing deployed unlearned models and could inform regulatory standards for machine unlearning verification, establishing representation-level checks as baseline requirements alongside output-level metrics.
